candidate_problems.text: What precise mechanism makes durable, shared records possible when causal structure itself is quantum? The conjecture centers on 'legibility' and repeatable interaction, but it leaves open the core explanatory problem: what microphysical structures or dynamics select stable memories, observer agreement, and recursively testable records without presupposing classical spacetime? This is significant because it could turn the vague requirement of emergent classicality into a concrete criterion for quantum gravity theories.

How can a unified theory define subsystem identity, repeated trials, and comparability of outcomes when spacetime is not fundamental? The conjecture assumes that physics requires re-identifying 'the same' system and comparing events across interactions, yet in quantum gravity the notions of localization, separability, and temporal ordering may all be emergent or approximate. The unresolved tension is whether these epistemic conditions are fundamental constraints on viable theories or merely effective features that arise late.

Is the proposed 'interaction-structure' incompatibility genuinely deeper than existing formulations of quantum gravity, or can it be reduced to known issues such as the problem of time, decoherence, relational observables, and information preservation? This is worth pursuing because the conjecture may either reveal a unifying meta-problem behind several disconnected technical obstacles or collapse into a redescription of them; distinguishing those possibilities would clarify whether it opens a genuinely new research program.
